skill.name: discord-bot-architect
skill.description: Specialized guidance for designing production-ready Discord bots with Discord.js and Pycord, including slash commands, intents, components, rate limits, and sharding.
skill.summary: Design production-ready Discord bots with Discord.js, Pycord, slash commands, components, and operational safeguards.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and critical patterns, but the reviewed file is a Markdown skill containing Discord bot examples rather than executable marketplace scripts. The critical heuristic and weak-crypto flags are false positives from combined example snippets and slash-command terminology. The main residual risk is that copied examples use dynamic local module loading, filesystem enumeration, and Discord token environment access, which are legitimate but require secure project hygiene.

medium_findings.title: Dynamic Local Module Loading in Example Bot Scaffolds
medium_findings.description: The Discord.js example enumerates local command and event folders, then requires each discovered file. The Pycord example similarly loads cogs from a local directory. This is a common bot architecture pattern, but copied projects must protect these directories because any writable file in them can execute during startup.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The dynamic loading behavior is explicit in the example code, and local module execution is real if users copy the scaffold. The risk is moderate because the source is instructional Markdown and the loaded paths are local project folders, not remote input.

low_findings.title: Discord Token Read from Environment in Examples
low_findings.description: The examples load dotenv and read DISCORD_TOKEN from environment variables. This is the recommended alternative to hardcoding secrets, but users should understand that bot tokens are sensitive and must not be logged, committed, or sent to third-party services.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment access is directly visible and tied to DISCORD_TOKEN. It is low risk in this context because the examples do not show exfiltration or logging of the token.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static external-command findings correspond to Markdown code fence delimiters around JavaScript and Python examples. No evidence found of Ruby backtick shell execution or command invocation in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown fences or truncated example boundaries, not executable shell syntax. The file is SKILL.md documentation, so this static category is a false positive.

low_findings.title: Slash Command Text Misclassified as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography findings appear to be triggered by slash-command terminology and unrelated example text. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, or other cryptographic algorithm use at the cited lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines shows Discord command descriptions and UI option text, not cryptographic functions. This is a strong false positive from pattern matching.

low_findings.title: External Documentation URL in Example Button
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL points to official Discord.js documentation as a link button example. This is normal instructional content and not evidence of data exfiltration or unsafe network behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is visible, static, and points to discord.js.org. There is no code path that sends credentials or user data to the URL.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Flags Are Contextual False Positives
low_findings.description: The system-reconnaissance findings occur on Discord intent or collector-related example lines. No evidence found of host enumeration, process discovery, network scanning, or platform fingerprinting.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Discord-specific bot permissions and interaction filtering. They do not inspect the local system or network environment.

low_findings.title: Combined Code, Network, and Credential Heuristic Dismissed
low_findings.description: The critical heuristic combines dynamic local loading, DISCORD_TOKEN environment access, and a documentation URL. Manual review found no data exfiltration, remote code loading, or prompt injection attempt.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The ingredients of the heuristic are present in example code, but their context is benign documentation. No reviewed line sends secrets to the external URL or fetches executable code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dynamic Require and Extension Loading
dangerous_patterns.description: Example code loads local JavaScript command files and Python cogs dynamically. This is legitimate for plugin-style bot architecture, but it becomes dangerous if attackers can write files into those folders.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dynamic module loading is clearly present and executable if copied into a bot project. The finding is not high confidence for malicious intent because it is a standard local extension pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sensitive Token Environment Access
dangerous_patterns.description: Example code reads DISCORD_TOKEN from environment variables. This is expected for Discord bots, but token handling remains sensitive and should be paired with secret-management guidance.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The token reads are explicit and semantically sensitive. There is no evidence of exfiltration, so this is a handling concern rather than malicious behavior.

content.value_statement: Discord bots often fail because they use weak command patterns, broad intents, or fragile runtime structure. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code toward safer Discord.js and Pycord bot architecture.
actual_capabilities: Provides Discord.js v14 and Pycord starter patterns for bot projects.
Explains slash command, component, and modal interaction structures.
Calls out privileged intent risks and recommends minimal gateway intents.
Highlights rate limit, command sync, heartbeat, and sharding concerns.
Shows local command, event, and cog organization patterns for maintainable bots.
limitations: The included code snippets are examples and may need completion before direct use.
It does not deploy or host Discord bots by itself.
It does not manage Discord Developer Portal settings automatically.
It assumes users protect bot tokens and local extension directories.

use_cases.title: Plan a New Community Bot
use_cases.description: Design command structure, required intents, and interaction flows before implementation.

use_cases.title: Refactor a Growing Bot
use_cases.description: Move commands, events, and cogs into maintainable modules with safer startup behavior.

use_cases.title: Review Production Readiness
use_cases.description: Check rate limits, token handling, privileged intents, and command registration practices.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Bot Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the discord-bot-architect skill to plan a small Discord bot with three slash commands, minimal intents, and safe token handling.

prompt_templates.title: Design Interactive Components
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the discord-bot-architect skill to design a Discord.js command that uses buttons and a select menu without enabling Message Content Intent.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor Bot Modules
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the discord-bot-architect skill to refactor my bot into command, event, and cog modules. Include security notes for dynamic loading.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Production Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the discord-bot-architect skill to review my Discord bot architecture for rate limits, sharding, command sync, intents, and token exposure risks.

output_examples.input: Plan a Discord.js moderation bot for a 20,000 member server.
output: Recommended minimal intents and slash-command structure.
A module layout for commands, events, and shared services.
Operational notes for command deployment, rate limits, and token storage.

output_examples.input: Review my Pycord bot for production readiness.
output: A checklist covering privileged intents, cogs, command sync, and heartbeat safety.
Specific warnings about blocking operations and environment-based secrets.

output_examples.input: Design a role-selection interaction with Discord components.
output: A recommended user flow using select menus and confirmation buttons.
Timeout and permission considerations for component collectors.
Notes about avoiding broad message permissions when slash commands are enough.

best_practices: Use slash commands and minimal gateway intents unless a feature clearly requires more access.
Store bot tokens in secret managers or environment variables and never commit them.
Deploy global command updates intentionally instead of syncing them on every startup.
anti_patterns: Do not enable Message Content Intent for ordinary command handling.
Do not let untrusted users write files into dynamically loaded command or cog folders.
Do not run blocking work inside gateway event handlers or interaction callbacks.

faq.question: Does this skill create a complete Discord bot?
faq.answer: It provides architecture guidance and example patterns. Users still need to complete, test, and deploy their bot.

faq.question: Does it support both JavaScript and Python?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill covers Discord.js for JavaScript and Pycord for Python.

faq.question: Can it help avoid privileged intents?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends minimal gateway intents and explains when privileged intents should be avoided.

faq.question: Is the skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: It is publishable with a medium-risk warning because examples include dynamic local module loading and token environment access.

faq.question: Does it handle Discord Developer Portal setup?
faq.answer: It can guide setup steps, but it does not configure the Developer Portal automatically.

faq.question: Can Claude Code or Codex use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
